William Zouzas

Biomedical engineer working across R&D / clinical engineering, human factors research, and medical device design — translating clinical need-finding into FDA-cleared devices and evidence-based user research.
zouzasw@gmail.com (978) 821-3565 LinkedIn ↗ Jump to projects ↓
01

About

I grew up chasing the same question in a lot of different labs: what does the person on the other end of this design actually need? That's taken me from interviewing lobstermen on a boat in Boston Harbor about their low back pain, to running driving simulator studies on ambulance drivers, to authoring the Design Verification protocol for an FDA-cleared surgical instrument.

I'm the co-founder of Engineer to Empower, a nonprofit helping girls become confident engineers through hands-on, community-based projects — an extension of the same user-centered instinct that drives my research and device work.

I live in Chelmsford, MA, with my family and four cats (Boo, Tuna, Mittens & Winnie). I'm Greek Orthodox, I bake more than is reasonable, and I'm always looking for the next place to hike. I'm also an avid reader and a bit of a foodie — always up for trying a new restaurant.

03

Projects

Filter by focus area. Click any project to expand the full write-up, data, and media.

Medical Device

Endoscopic Suture Cutter — Co-Inventor

EnVision Endoscopy, Inc. · Waltham, MA
2024
FDA-cleared
▶

Co-invented and led the design and regulatory development of a novel Class II endoscopic suture cutter that received FDA 510(k) clearance in December 2024. Built detailed CAD models with GD&T, compiled a 50-page feasibility report, authored all Design Verification test methods and the DV test protocol, and tracked design history in the Greenlight Guru QMS. Also authored the Instructions for Use (IFU) manual — warnings, precautions, labeled diagrams — for an FDA pre-submission covering 3 novel endoscopic devices.

STATUS 510(k) cleared
PATENT US 12,569,244
ROLE Co-inventor, lead design engineer
View granted patent ↗
Skills: CAD/GD&T · Design Verification & Test Protocols · 510(k) submission · IFU authorship · Greenlight Guru QMS · Design Inputs/Outputs
Medical DeviceHuman Factors

Lobster Trap Hauling System to Reduce Musculoskeletal Injuries — Capstone, Team Lead

University of Massachusetts Lowell · Mentor: Dr. Bryan Buchholz · PM: Scott Fulmer
Jan – Dec 2021
▶

Led a team of biomedical engineering students to design a novel roller-conveyor system reducing musculoskeletal disorders in Northeast lobstermen, who haul 50–70 lb. traps hundreds of times daily. Conducted field research — including a lobster boat outing in Boston Harbor to interview lobstermen and observe hauling technique — to define 23 system requirements grounded in OSHA maritime standards and ergonomics literature. Designed the roller-conveyor system in SolidWorks and built a working prototype from wood, aluminum, and anodized steel with the help of campus carpenters.

TEAM 5 students, 1 mentor, 1 PM
DELIVERABLE 23 system requirements · field-tested prototype
Featured UML student profile ↗
Skills: Ergonomic risk assessment · Field research & contextual inquiry · SolidWorks · Requirements definition · Team leadership
Medical Device

Dialysis Systems Testing & Sensor Analysis

NxStage Medical, Inc. (Fresenius Medical Care) · Systems Engineering Dept.
Jul 2020 – Jan 2021
▶

Ran test procedures on dialysis machines in collaboration with Disposables, Electrical, Mechanical, and Software teams; collected and analyzed blood leak detector (BLD) sensor data across multiple systems using Excel and Minitab (regression modeling to support sensor threshold development), largely with minimal oversight. Independently developed and ran a final project investigating rinse-back system efficiency across Dialysis Laboratory Testing and NxStage System One platforms, presenting results and recommendations to the Systems Engineering team.

FOCUS Blood leak detector sensor thresholds
METHOD Regression modeling, t-tests (Minitab)
Skills: Test procedure development · Statistical analysis (Minitab, Excel) · Cross-functional collaboration · Technical reporting
Human Factors

A Scoping Review of Soft Robot User Interfaces — 1st Author

Healthcare Ergonomics Analytics Lab (HEAL), Purdue University · Co-PIs: Dr. Denny Yu, Dr. Laura Blumenschein · NSF M3X Program
Jul 2024 – Aug 2025
▶

Led a scoping review to understand how often user interfaces are implemented for human control of soft robots, how soft-robot characteristics shape interface design, and how often (and how well) user studies evaluate them. Reviewed 137 included papers and found user-interface design significantly underdeveloped in soft robotics, with fewer than half of studies including any user evaluation — and those that did leaned heavily on objective over subjective measures. Delivered as a lecture at IISE 2025 and part of an NSF-funded collaboration with Mechanical Engineering.

N 137 included papers
STATUS Manuscript submitted, 2025–2026
Skills: PRISMA-ScR methodology · Systematic/scoping review design · Data extraction & synthesis · Scientific writing & presentation
Human Factors

Human Learning of Soft Robot Motion & Control — Co-First Author

Healthcare Ergonomics Analytics Lab (HEAL), Purdue University · Co-PIs: Dr. Denny Yu, Dr. Laura Blumenschein
Jan – May 2025
▶

Led a pilot user study on how teaching method (expert demo vs. self-guided) and task order (easy→hard vs. hard→easy) affect novice users learning to control an inflatable cable-driven soft robot. Assessed workload and usability with NASA-TLX, System Usability Scale, and the Godspeed Questionnaire Series, alongside task completion time, success rate, eye tracking, and heart-rate variability. Published as a co-first-authored paper at IEEE RoboSoft 2026.

N 12 participants
FINDING Task order > teaching method in effect size
PUBLISHED IEEE RoboSoft 2026 (Kanazawa, Japan)
Read the paper (DOI) ↗
Skills: IRB protocol design · Eye tracking (Tobii Pro Glasses 2) · Polar H10 HR monitoring · NASA-TLX/SUS/Godspeed · Mentoring undergraduate researchers
Human FactorsMedical Device

Picture vs. Video Instructions for Needle Loading & Unloading — Master's Project

Northeastern University / EnVision Endoscopy, Inc. · Advisors: Dr. Azadeh Khanicheh, Dr. Yingzi Lin
Sep – Dec 2023
▶

Ran a between-subjects simulated-use study with 10 surgeons, fellows, and nurses at Brigham and Women's Hospital to evaluate picture vs. video instructions for needle loading/unloading — critical, error-prone tasks in the EnVision Endoscopy Suturing System. Measured task completion time, number of attempts, and error frequency; identified 6 distinct error types and used participant feedback to redesign the picture instructions with multi-angle views, added text, and error-mitigation steps for clinical implementation. Full study details remain confidential to EnVision Endoscopy.

N 10 clinicians
RESULT Video: 4.2 min vs. picture: 6.4 min (p = 0.0398)
OUTCOME 6 error types identified → IFU redesign
Skills: Simulated-use / usability testing · IFU redesign · Between-subjects study design · Clinical site coordination
Human Factors

Siren Noise & Visual-Manual Distraction on Cognitive Workload During Simulated Driving

Northeastern University · Intelligent Human Machine Systems (IHMS) Lab, Dr. Yingzi Lin
Jan – Apr 2023
▶

A continuation of the EMT siren work, moved into a driving simulator to study ambulance-relevant cognitive workload (CW) under three conditions: driving alone, driving with a visual-manual distractor, and driving with both distractor and siren noise. Collected and analyzed respiration rate (RR) and skin conductance (SC) as physiological CW measures, plus lane deviation, speed variation, and NASA-TLX.

N 6 participants
RR Siren+distraction > distraction-only > control (F = 6.426, p = 0.010)
SPEED VAR. Same ordering (F = 4.184, p = 0.036)
Skills: Physiological data collection (RR, SC) · Driving simulation · NASA-TLX · Repeated-measures ANOVA
Human Factors

The Effect of Siren Noise on Task Performance & Stress Levels of EMTs

Northeastern University · with Elizabeth Roe · in collaboration with NUEMS
Sep – Dec 2022
▶

Investigated whether siren noise affects EMT task performance and stress, and whether any performance effect is mediated by stress. Seven EMTs from Northeastern University EMS answered scenario-based NREMT practice questions with and without siren noise, while heart-rate change, accuracy, and response time were recorded. Participants reported feeling more distracted, stressed, and anxious with siren noise — but performance was not correlated with stress in the way hypothesized.

N 7 EMTs (NUEMS)
RESULT No sig. difference siren vs. no-siren
FINDING Siren condition: ↑ HR change, ↑ accuracy, faster response
Skills: Within-subjects design · Physiological monitoring (HR) · Field collaboration with EMS · Statistical hypothesis testing
Human Factors

Evaluating the Usefulness of Wearing an Exoskeleton During Lifting — Honors College Research Fellowship

NERVE Center, University of Massachusetts Lowell
Feb 2019 – Aug 2020
▶

Collected physiological data — heart rate, surface EMG, and VO₂ max — from 12 participants across weekly 5-hour sessions to study the effects of exoskeleton-assisted lifting on the body. Built hands-on expertise in biomechanics research using the Qualisys Motion Capture System: marker placement, multi-camera setup and calibration, and kinematic analysis in Qualisys Track Manager (QTM).

N 12 participants
INSTRUMENTATION sEMG · HR · VO₂ max · Qualisys mocap
Skills: Motion capture (Qualisys/QTM) · Surface EMG · Biomechanics data collection · Long-session participant coordination
Outreach

Engineer to Empower, Inc. — Co-Founder, Board Member & Outreach Director

engineer2empower.com ↗
Apr 2024 – Present
▶

Co-founded a nonprofit building a community where girls can pursue engineering with confidence, through hands-on, community-based projects. Co-created the mission, vision, and values; designed and trademarked the logo (USPTO); and built/maintain the organization's website on GoDaddy. Facilitated partnerships with the Lawrence Lin MakerSpace at UMass Lowell (equipment & space for the first program), Mill City Grows (planter-building project for 40 third- and fourth-graders at Lincoln Elementary, Lowell), and Dear Women in STEM (organized a virtual Ph.D.-student panel on navigating industry and academia). Now expanding programming to the Museum of Science, Boston — kicking off with an Adaptive Eating Utensil workshop at the Museum's Disability Pride Celebration on July 25, 2026.

REACH 200+ students since 2024
PROGRAMMING 20+ workshop sessions held
NEW PARTNER Museum of Science, Boston — Disability Pride Celebration, Jul 25 2026
TRADEMARKS "E2E" & "Engineer to Empower" (USPTO)
Skills: Nonprofit governance · Brand/trademark development · Partnership development · Program design · Community outreach

Secretary, Human Factors and Ergonomics Society (HFES)

Purdue University Chapter
Jul 2024 – May 2025

Outreach

Light in Medicine — Only Photons Can Do That — Project-Based Learning Design

UMass Lowell UTeach / Methuen High School · Mentors: Dr. Sumudu Lewis, Dr. Walfre Franco, Dina Katz
Jan – Apr 2022
▶

As part of a STEM Teaching (UTeach) minor and pre-practicum at Methuen High School, designed 6 project-based lesson plans teaching high schoolers how light is used in biomedical applications — from spectrophotometry and pulse oximetry to building microscopes, telescopes, and kaleidoscopes. Culminated in a hands-on field-trip workshop at UMass Lowell led with Dr. Walfre Franco (Biomedical Engineering). Supported by an SPIE Education Outreach Grant; won the 2022 Student Research and Community Engagement Symposium and presented as a poster at the UTeach STEM Educators Conference.

LESSONS 6, plus field-trip workshop
AWARD 2022 Symposium Winner · UTeach Conference poster
UML news feature ↗
Skills: Curriculum design · Public speaking & facilitation · Grant-supported programming · STEM outreach

Also on record

Designing & Testing Face Shield Designs for Healthcare Workers (COVID-19)

UMass Lowell, Apr–May 2020 — compared PETG vs. ABS 3D-printed face shields using SolidWorks SimulationXpress; optimized geometry cut mass and von Mises stress, with fillets reducing peak stress significantly.

Diabetic Retinopathy Detection — Team Lead

UMass Lowell, Jan–Apr 2022 — led a team building an automated classifier for diabetic retinopathy severity in eye images, to reduce manual clinician labeling.

Applying the Engineering Design Process to Assistive Technology

UMass Lowell UTeach pre-practicum, Sep–Dec 2021 — designed lesson plans on assistive-device design, inspired by eNable Lowell, for a Methuen HS engineering classroom.

3D-Printed Assistive Lacrosse/Hockey Device

UMass Lowell, 2019–2021 — designed and 3D-printed an adaptive sports device (SolidWorks, 3D printing).
